spec_match.rationale: The page now models the implementation change, privacy motivation, collision pressure, and exhaustion branches, but it remains a faithful simulation because web content cannot directly read Chromium's native TCP source-port allocator.

interactive.rationale: The concept has a multi-control live simulator with range presets, held-port pressure, hidden-tab connections, retry budget, fallback slots, per-strategy allocation output, and 100-burst aggregation.

blurb_alignment.rationale: The page delivers the feature page's framing: Windows Chromium switches from predictable sequential source ports to randomized allocation with no web-facing API change.

edge_cases.rationale: The simulator exposes narrow ranges, heavy TIME_WAIT pressure, reserved-port patterns, randomized retry collisions, sequential skip behavior, connection-pool fallback, and failed-connect exhaustion.

references.rationale: The required chromestatus.com/feature/5106900286570496 link remains present.

design_system.rationale: The updated page uses shared CSS variables for colors, pairs button background overrides with explicit text colors, and replaces the static note with live controls and output.

openQuestions.title: Verify retry-budget wording against Chromium's implementation notes
openQuestions.detail: The simulator intentionally exposes a simplified random retry budget so reuse pressure and fallback branches are visible. If a future implementation note documents the exact native retry strategy, align the labels and default value with that wording.

summary: The blocking critique has been addressed: the page is now a genuinely interactive allocator simulator that compares sequential and randomized behavior, demonstrates the hidden-tab counting motivation, and covers collision, range, fallback, and exhaustion branches. The only remaining caveat is that the native Windows TCP allocator is represented by an explanatory model rather than a directly observable browser API.
